schools.summary: Gill: since the persons the apostle writes to were born again and therefore ought to love one another, he exhorts them to the disuse of such vices as were disagreeable to their character as regenerate men and contrary to brotherly love; he dissuades them from them and advises to lay them aside; Peter charging that they lay aside all malice, guile, hypocrisies, envies, and evil speakings — the putting off of the sins that war against love (Peter, following the call to fervent love, bidding them strip off, 'laying aside' as one casts off a filthy garment, the vices that destroy love and disgrace the new birth: 'all malice,' ill-will and spite, 'and all guile,' deceit and craft, 'and hypocrisies,' false pretence, 'and envies,' grudging others' good, 'and all evil speakings,' slanders and backbitings; these all being sins of the tongue and heart that break the bond of brotherly love, and the regenerate man, born of the incorruptible seed, must put them all away as inconsistent with his new nature, so holiness begins with the laying aside of all that opposes love).

themes: wherefore laying aside all malice, and all guile, and hypocrisies
and envies, and all evil speakings
the putting off of the sins that war against love
casting off what is inconsistent with the new nature

schools.summary: Gill: the phrase describes the persons addressed, regenerate ones lately born again, and carries an exhortation to desire the word with the eagerness with which a newborn babe desires its mother's milk (the Syriac rendering 'be ye simple as infants'); Peter charging that, as newborn babes, they desire the sincere milk of the word, that they may grow by it — the eager appetite for the pure word as the means of spiritual growth (Peter, having bidden them lay aside corrupt things, now giving the positive nourishment: 'as newborn babes,' those lately born again, 'desire the sincere milk of the word,' long for, with the keen natural craving of an infant for milk, the pure, unadulterated word of God, 'that ye may grow thereby'; the aptness of the figure, that the word is the milk that nourishes the new-born soul, 'sincere,' pure and unmixed with error or human corruption, and the babe must crave it earnestly, for by it and by no other food the soul grows up in grace, so the believer's spiritual growth depends on a hearty appetite for the pure word of God).

themes: as newborn babes, desire the sincere milk of the word
that ye may grow thereby
the appetite for the pure word as the means of growth
the word the milk that nourishes the new-born soul

schools.summary: Gill: reference is had to 'O taste and see that the Lord is good' (Psalm 34:8); by the Lord is meant the Lord Jesus Christ, who is gracious and amiable and lovely in his person, who has a fulness of grace; Peter adding, 'if so be ye have tasted that the Lord is gracious' — the experimental taste of Christ's grace that whets the appetite for his word (Peter grounding the craving for the word in a sweet experience already had: 'if so be ye have tasted that the Lord is gracious,' as those who have once tasted, by faith, the graciousness, sweetness, and kindness of the Lord Jesus; the force of the figure, that having tasted how gracious the Lord is, the soul longs for more of him in his word, even as one who has tasted a sweet food desires it again, so the appetite for the word springs from a real, personal experience of Christ's grace, and the more the believer has tasted of his sweetness, the more he craves the milk of the word in which Christ is found).

themes: if so be ye have tasted
that the Lord is gracious
the taste of Christ's grace whetting the appetite
longing for more of him in his word

schools.summary: Gill: Christ is here, as often elsewhere, compared to a 'stone,' and Peter, by the use of this metaphor, shows that he is not the rock but Christ is the rock on which the church is built, and he is the foundation stone on which every believer is laid; Peter describing Christ as a living stone, to whom they come, disallowed of men but chosen of God and precious — the coming to Christ the living stone, rejected by men, prized by God (Peter setting Christ before them under the figure of a stone: 'to whom coming,' drawing near to Christ by faith, 'as unto a living stone,' a stone yet alive, for the foundation of the church is no dead rock but the living Christ who has life in himself and gives it, and marking the double estimate of this stone: 'disallowed indeed of men,' rejected and cast aside by men as the builders refused him, 'but chosen of God, and precious'; the contrast that runs through the passage, what men despised God chose and prized as precious, so the believer comes to the very stone the world rejected, finding in him the living and chosen foundation that God himself esteems).

themes: to whom coming, as unto a living stone
disallowed indeed of men, but chosen of God, and precious
Christ the living stone, rejected by men, prized by God
the believer coming to the stone the world rejected

schools.summary: Gill: saints are compared to stones; they lie in the same quarry and are the same by nature as the rest of mankind, till dug out and separated from thence by the powerful and efficacious grace of God, when they are hewn and made fit for the spiritual building; Peter declaring that they also, as living stones, are built up a spiritual house and holy priesthood to offer spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God by Christ — the believers as living stones, a spiritual temple and priesthood offering spiritual sacrifices (Peter extending the figure from Christ to his people: 'ye also, as lively stones,' made living by union with the living Stone Christ, 'are built up a spiritual house,' the church, a living temple of God raised on Christ the foundation, 'an holy priesthood,' every believer a priest not of one tribe only, 'to offer up spiritual sacrifices, acceptable to God by Jesus Christ'; the rich truth, that believers are both the temple and its priests, offering not animal sacrifices but 'spiritual' ones, praise, prayer, thanksgiving, a broken heart, good works, themselves, and these accepted by God only 'by Jesus Christ,' through whom alone all worship is made pleasing).

themes: ye also, as lively stones, are built up a spiritual house, an holy priesthood
to offer up spiritual sacrifices
acceptable to God by Jesus Christ
believers both the temple and its priests

schools.summary: Gill: this is produced as a proof of the excellency of Christ as compared to a stone, of his usefulness in the spiritual building, of his being chosen of God and precious though rejected by men, and of the happiness, comfort, and safety of those that believe; Peter citing the scripture (Isaiah 28:16), 'Behold, I lay in Sion a chief corner stone, elect, precious: and he that believeth on him shall not be confounded' — the prophetic warrant for Christ the chosen corner stone and the believer's security (Peter backing his teaching by Isaiah's prophecy: God himself says, 'Behold, I lay in Sion a chief corner stone,' Christ laid by God as the great corner-stone that binds and upholds the whole building of the church, 'elect, precious,' chosen and prized by God, and adding the promise to faith: 'and he that believeth on him shall not be confounded,' shall never be put to shame, disappointed, or made ashamed of his trust; the comfort, that the one who builds his hope on Christ, God's chosen corner stone, rests on a sure foundation and shall never be confounded).

themes: Behold, I lay in Sion a chief corner stone, elect, precious
and he that believeth on him shall not be confounded
the prophetic warrant for Christ the corner stone
the believer resting on a sure foundation

schools.summary: Gill: those who believe are not all that can say their creed or give their assent to a divine revelation and that the Scriptures are the word of God, but such as have a true and spiritual faith, to whom Christ is precious; Peter declaring that to those who believe Christ is precious, but to the disobedient the rejected stone is made the head of the corner — the dividing of men by their response to the precious yet rejected stone (Peter showing how Christ divides mankind: 'unto you therefore which believe he is precious,' to true believers Christ being altogether precious, prized above all as he is to God, 'but unto them which be disobedient,' the unbelieving and disobedient, 'the stone which the builders disallowed, the same is made the head of the corner'; the solemn truth, that the very stone that men, the builders, rejected and cast away, God has exalted to be 'the head of the corner,' the chief place in the building, so Christ, despised by the disobedient, is set by God in the highest honour and is precious to all who believe).

themes: unto you therefore which believe he is precious
but unto them which be disobedient
the stone which the builders disallowed, the same is made the head of the corner
Christ dividing mankind by their response

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle alludes to the prophecy of the Messiah (Isaiah 8:14), which had its accomplishment in the unbelieving and disobedient Jews, who stumbled at his birth and parentage, at the manner of his birth (being born of a virgin), at the meanness of his person and condition, and at his doctrine; Peter declaring that Christ is a stone of stumbling and rock of offence to the disobedient who stumble at the word, to which they were appointed — Christ a stumbling-stone to the disobedient, who were appointed to it (Peter completing the contrast: the same Christ who is precious to believers is, to the unbelieving, 'a stone of stumbling, and a rock of offence,' they stumbling and falling over him 'even to them which stumble at the word, being disobedient,' their stumbling springing from their disobedience to the gospel, and adding a solemn word: 'whereunto also they were appointed'; the Reformed taking the clause of God's just judicial appointment of the reprobate to the ruin their unbelief deserves, while all agree the stumbling itself is the fruit of their own disobedience).

debates.question: What does 'whereunto also they were appointed' mean?

sides.position: The disobedient were appointed, in God's righteous sovereign ordination, to the stumbling and ruin that their unbelief deserves — a judicial appointment of the reprobate
The appointment is to the consequence of stumbling; the stumbling itself springs from their own disobedience, not God's compulsion
holders: all agree the stumbling is the fruit of disobedience

themes: and a stone of stumbling, and a rock of offence
even to them which stumble at the word, being disobedient
whereunto also they were appointed
Christ a stumbling-stone to the disobedient

schools.summary: Gill: the allusion throughout is to the people of Israel in general, who in an external way were all that is here said; the believers are this in a spiritual sense — 'a chosen generation,' or kindred, a race chosen by God, 'a royal priesthood,' kings and priests unto God, 'an holy nation,' a people set apart and sanctified, 'a peculiar people,' God's own special possession; Peter declaring that they are these, to show forth the praises of him who called them from darkness to light — the high titles of the church and the purpose of their calling (Peter, against the dark lot of the disobedient, heaping up the glorious titles of believers, and naming the great end of all this dignity: 'that ye should shew forth the praises of him who hath called you out of darkness into his marvellous light'; the believers' high standing being for a purpose, to proclaim and display the excellencies of the God who called them out of the darkness of sin and ignorance into the marvellous light of his grace and truth).

themes: but ye are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, an holy nation, a peculiar people
that ye should shew forth the praises of him
who hath called you out of darkness into his marvellous light
the high titles of the church and their purpose

schools.summary: Gill: God's elect, whether among Jews or Gentiles, were from eternity his chosen people and his covenant people, and as such were given to Christ and became his people, his care and charge; but in time past, before conversion, they were not a people, a 'Loammi' (not my people, Hosea 1:9-10) being put upon them; Peter declaring that in time past they were not a people, but are now the people of God, and had not obtained mercy, but now have obtained mercy — the great change from being no people to God's people through mercy (Peter, recalling Hosea's prophecy, marking the wonderful change grace has wrought: 'which in time past were not a people,' before they were no people of God, outside his covenant, especially the Gentiles, 'but are now the people of God,' now openly owned and made his people, 'which had not obtained mercy, but now have obtained mercy'; the double turn, from 'not a people' to 'the people of God' and from 'not obtained mercy' to 'obtained mercy,' so their whole new standing rests on the free mercy of God, who took those who were no people and not pitied and made them his own and the objects of his mercy).

themes: which in time past were not a people, but are now the people of God
which had not obtained mercy, but now have obtained mercy
the change from no people to God's people
their whole new standing resting on free mercy

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle, from characters of the saints which express their blessings and privileges, passes to exhortations to duties; he addresses the saints under the affectionate appellation 'dearly beloved' to express his great affection; Peter beseeching them, as strangers and pilgrims, to abstain from fleshly lusts which war against the soul — the pilgrim's call to abstain from the soul-warring lusts of the flesh (Peter, turning from privilege to duty, making a tender appeal grounded in their pilgrim character: 'dearly beloved, I beseech you as strangers and pilgrims,' as those who are but sojourners here, not citizens of this world, their home being heaven, 'abstain from fleshly lusts,' keep wholly away from the sinful desires of the flesh, 'which war against the soul'; the reason that gives weight to the appeal, that these fleshly lusts are not harmless indulgences but enemies that 'war against the soul,' waging a deadly campaign against the believer's spiritual life and welfare, so the pilgrim, bound for a better country, must hold himself apart from the lusts that fight against his very soul).

themes: dearly beloved, I beseech you as strangers and pilgrims, abstain from fleshly lusts
which war against the soul
the pilgrim's call to abstain from fleshly lusts
lusts that war against the soul, not harmless indulgences

schools.summary: Gill: to have the conversation honest is to provide things honest in the sight of men, to live and walk honestly before all, to do those things which are right and honest in the sight of God and among men, and to order the conversation aright; Peter charging that their conduct among the Gentiles be honest, that the heathen, who speak against them as evildoers, may by their good works glorify God in the day of visitation — the honest life that silences slander and wins the heathen to glorify God (Peter bidding the believers, living among unbelieving Gentiles, keep 'your conversation honest,' a manner of life upright, honourable, and good before all, and naming the gracious effect: 'that, whereas they speak against you as evildoers,' though the heathen slander and accuse the Christians falsely, 'they may by your good works, which they shall behold, glorify God in the day of visitation'; the hope held out, that the very heathen who now revile them, seeing the believers' good and honourable works, may be brought to acknowledge and glorify God when his grace visits them, so a holy, honest life is a witness that may turn slanderers into worshippers).

themes: having your conversation honest among the Gentiles
whereas they speak against you as evildoers
they may by your good works... glorify God in the day of visitation
the honest life turning slanderers to worshippers

schools.summary: Gill: 'every ordinance of man' is 'every human creation' or institution, not 'all the sons of men' or all individuals (for some are in such stations that they are not to be submitted to but to be ruled), but the civil ordinances and authorities appointed among men; Peter charging that they submit to every ordinance of man for the Lord's sake, whether to the king as supreme — the duty of submission to civil authority out of obedience to the Lord (Peter turning to the believers' duty toward the state: 'submit yourselves to every ordinance of man,' yield obedience to the lawful institutions and authorities of human government, 'for the Lord's sake,' from a right motive, not from fear or constraint only but out of obedience and reverence to the Lord who has ordained government, 'whether it be to the king, as supreme'; the breadth and ground of the duty, that submission is due to the whole order of civil authority, beginning with the highest, 'the king, as supreme,' and rendered 'for the Lord's sake,' so obedience to lawful magistrates is part of the Christian's obedience to God himself).

themes: submit yourselves to every ordinance of man for the Lord's sake
whether it be to the king, as supreme
submission to civil authority for the Lord's sake
obedience to magistrates part of obedience to God

schools.summary: Gill: 'governors' are inferior magistrates, such as were under the Roman emperor, proconsuls and procurators, such as Pontius Pilate, Felix, and Festus, who had under the emperor the government of particular nations, provinces, and cities; Peter directing submission also to governors, sent for the punishment of evildoers and the praise of those who do well — the submission due to magistrates and the God-given purpose of government (Peter, having named the king, extending submission to the lower ranks: 'or unto governors,' the subordinate officers under the supreme power, and giving the divine purpose of all such authority: 'as unto them that are sent by him for the punishment of evildoers, and for the praise of them that do well'; the twofold end of government as God ordained it, to restrain and punish the wicked, 'the punishment of evildoers,' and to encourage and commend the good, 'the praise of them that do well,' so, since rulers are sent for this good and God-appointed end, the believer is rightly to submit to them, doing well and so winning their approval rather than their punishment).

themes: or unto governors, as unto them that are sent by him
for the punishment of evildoers, and for the praise of them that do well
submission to magistrates and government's God-given purpose
punishing the wicked, commending the good

schools.summary: Gill: 'so is the will of God' refers chiefly to what follows, that with well doing they may put to silence the ignorance of foolish men (though it is also a truth that it is God's will men should be subject to magistrates); Barnes: it is the will of God that by upright conduct his people should silence calumny; Peter declaring that it is the will of God that by well doing they put to silence the ignorance of foolish men — the silencing of slander by a life of well-doing (Peter giving the divine reason for the believers' good and submissive conduct: 'for so is the will of God,' this being what God wills and requires, 'that with well doing,' by a life of doing good and right, 'ye may put to silence the ignorance of foolish men'; the gracious strategy, that the believers' answer to slander is not retaliation in words but a manner of life so upright that it stops the mouths of their accusers, the 'foolish men' who in their 'ignorance' speak against the Christians being best refuted and silenced not by argument but by good works that leave nothing to be said against them, so a holy life is God's appointed way to silence ungodly slander).

themes: for so is the will of God
that with well doing ye may put to silence the ignorance of foolish men
silencing slander by a life of well-doing
the answer to slander not retaliation but upright living

schools.summary: Gill: these converted Jews might value themselves on their freedom, partly as the descendants of Abraham and so freeborn and not to be brought into bondage, and chiefly because of the liberty they had in and by Christ; the apostle allows they were freemen but warns of the abuse of it; Peter charging that, as free, they not use their liberty as a cloak of maliciousness, but as the servants of God — the true freedom that does not abuse liberty but serves God (Peter guarding against a misuse of Christian liberty in the matter of submission: the believers are indeed 'free,' made free by Christ, not slaves of men or of the law, yet they are 'not [to use] your liberty for a cloke of maliciousness,' not to make their freedom a pretext or cover for doing evil, disorder, or shaking off lawful authority, 'but as the servants of God'; the paradox of gospel freedom, that the believer is free yet a servant of God, and his very liberty is to be used in willing obedience to God not as an excuse for license, so true Christian freedom never cloaks sin or rebellion but expresses itself in glad service to God).

themes: as free, and not using your liberty for a cloke of maliciousness
but as the servants of God
the right use of Christian liberty
free yet servants, never cloaking sin

schools.summary: Gill: 'honour all men' means giving honour to whom honour is due, according to the place, station, and circumstances in which they are, the gifts of providence and grace bestowed on them, and the usefulness they are of, whether Jews or Gentiles, rich or poor, believers or unbelievers; Peter charging them to honour all men, love the brotherhood, fear God, and honour the king — the fourfold compass of Christian duty toward all, the saints, God, and the king (Peter summing up the believer's social and religious duties in four brief pointed commands: 'honour all men,' render to every man the respect due to him as a fellow-creature made in God's image, 'love the brotherhood,' a special warm love to the whole family of believers, 'fear God,' a reverent holy fear and worship of God above all, and 'honour the king,' give due respect to the civil ruler; the order and proportion, that honour is due to all men but love to the brethren, fear to God alone and honour to the king, so each receives its proper measure, general honour to all, special love to the saints, supreme fear to God, and civil honour to the ruler).

themes: honour all men. Love the brotherhood
Fear God. Honour the king
the fourfold compass of Christian duty
general honour, special love, supreme fear, civil honour

schools.summary: Gill: some Jews held that, being the seed of Abraham, they ought not to be the servants of any, and particularly believers in Christ thought they ought not to serve unbelieving masters, nor even believing ones; against this Peter charges submission; Peter charging servants to be subject to their masters with all fear, not only the good and gentle but also the froward — the submission of servants required even toward harsh and unjust masters (Peter turning to the duty of Christian servants, a large class then: 'servants, be subject to your masters with all fear,' yield a dutiful, reverent obedience to those over them, and extending it to the hardest case: 'not only to the good and gentle,' the kind and reasonable masters easy to serve, 'but also to the froward,' the harsh, perverse, and unjust ones; the searching demand, that Christian submission is not to be measured by the master's character, withheld from the cruel and given only to the kind, but rendered even to the difficult and unfair master for conscience toward God, so the servant's duty stands firm whatever the master's temper).

themes: servants, be subject to your masters with all fear
not only to the good and gentle, but also to the froward
the submission of servants even to harsh masters
submission not measured by the master's character

schools.summary: Gill: 'this is thankworthy,' or 'grace,' a fruit and effect of grace, an instance of it in which it shows itself; the Syriac and Alexandrian copy add 'with God,' that is, this is grateful and acceptable to God; Peter declaring that it is thankworthy if a man for conscience toward God endure grief, suffering wrongfully — the grace of patiently bearing unjust suffering for God's sake (Peter giving the principle that ennobles the servant's hard lot: 'for this is thankworthy,' a thing of grace, acceptable and pleasing to God, 'if a man for conscience toward God endure grief, suffering wrongfully'; the two marks that make such suffering acceptable, first the cause, that it must be suffered 'wrongfully,' the man being innocent and suffering unjustly not for his own faults, and second the motive, 'for conscience toward God,' borne out of regard to God and a tender conscience not mere necessity, so that when a believer endures undeserved grief patiently, out of conscience toward God, it is no mean thing but a grace well-pleasing in God's sight).

themes: for this is thankworthy
if a man for conscience toward God endure grief, suffering wrongfully
the grace of bearing unjust suffering for God
innocent suffering borne out of conscience toward God

schools.summary: Barnes: 'what glory is it,' what honour or credit would it be, if when ye be buffeted for your faults, that is if you are punished when you deserve it, you take it patiently; Gill notes the faults are such as are truly committed and the man is guilty of; Peter asking what glory there is in patiently bearing deserved punishment, but declaring that to suffer patiently for well-doing is acceptable with God — the acceptable patience that bears suffering for well-doing not for faults (Peter sharpening the point: there is no special credit in enduring deserved punishment patiently, 'for what glory is it, if, when ye be buffeted for your faults, ye shall take it patiently?' if a man is beaten for real wrongdoing and bears it, that is only what justice requires, no glory, 'but if, when ye do well, and suffer for it, ye take it patiently, this is acceptable with God'; the crucial distinction, that patience under deserved chastening is no virtue but patience under undeserved suffering, when one has done well and yet is made to suffer, is the very thing 'acceptable with God,' a true grace that pleases him, so the glory lies not in suffering as such but in patiently suffering for righteousness).

themes: for what glory is it, if, when ye be buffeted for your faults, ye shall take it patiently
but if, when ye do well, and suffer for it, ye take it patiently
this is acceptable with God
the glory in suffering patiently for righteousness

schools.summary: Gill: they were called both to well doing, of which none but the effectually called are capable and which they are fitted for and obliged to perform, and to suffer for so doing, which they must always expect, and to patience in suffering for it; Peter declaring that to this they were called, because Christ also suffered for us, leaving an example that we should follow his steps — the believer's calling to suffer patiently after Christ's own example (Peter grounding the duty of patient suffering in the believer's very calling and in Christ's example: 'for even hereunto were ye called,' Christians being called not only to do well but to suffer for it patiently, 'because Christ also suffered for us, leaving us an example, that ye should follow his steps'; the two truths joined here, that Christ 'suffered for us,' his suffering being first and chiefly redemptive, for our sakes, a thing we cannot imitate, and yet also 'leaving us an example,' in the manner of his suffering, his patience and meekness, he set a pattern we are to 'follow,' treading in his very footsteps, so the suffering Christian looks to his suffering Lord, both as Saviour and as model).

themes: for even hereunto were ye called: because Christ also suffered for us
leaving us an example
that ye should follow his steps
Christ both Saviour and model in suffering

schools.summary: Gill: Christ was in the likeness of sinful flesh; he looked like a sinful man, being born of a sinful woman and keeping company with sinful men, being himself a man of sorrows greatly afflicted and at last put to death, and was traduced as a sinner by his enemies and had all the sins of his people imputed to him; yet he did no sin; Peter declaring that Christ did no sin, neither was guile found in his mouth — the spotless innocence of Christ amid his unjust suffering (Peter, setting forth Christ as the pattern of patient suffering, first marking his perfect sinlessness, the ground of his being the supreme example of suffering wrongfully: 'who did no sin,' in his whole life committing not one act of sin, perfectly holy in deed, 'neither was guile found in his mouth,' no deceit or falsehood in his words, perfectly pure in speech as in deed; the force for the argument, that Christ, though utterly innocent in act and word, suffered the cruellest wrongs, so the believers, who suffer though sinful, have in their sinless Lord the highest model of innocent suffering patiently borne, and no ground to complain when they suffer who are not wholly innocent as he was).

themes: who did no sin
neither was guile found in his mouth
the spotless innocence of the suffering Christ
the sinless Lord the model of innocent suffering

schools.summary: Gill: when Christ was reviled and reproached as a glutton, a winebibber, a friend of publicans and sinners, all the reply he made was that Wisdom is justified of her children, and when charged with casting out devils by Beelzebub the prince of devils he defended himself meekly and made no railing reply; Peter declaring that Christ, when reviled, reviled not again, and when he suffered, threatened not, but committed himself to him that judges righteously — the perfect meekness of Christ, who answered wrong not with revenge but with trust in God (Peter drawing out the manner of Christ's suffering as the pattern for the believer: 'who, when he was reviled, reviled not again,' when bitterly reproached and insulted returning no insult, 'when he suffered, he threatened not,' when made to suffer uttering no threats of revenge against his persecutors, 'but committed himself to him that judgeth righteously'; the secret of this meekness, that Christ neither retaliated nor threatened but 'committed himself,' and his cause, into the hands of God, 'him that judgeth righteously,' trusting the righteous Judge to vindicate him in his own time, so the believer, wronged and reviled, is to follow Christ in answering not with revenge but with patient trust in the righteous God).

themes: who, when he was reviled, reviled not again; when he suffered, he threatened not
but committed himself to him that judgeth righteously
Christ's perfect meekness under wrong
answering not with revenge but with trust in God

schools.summary: Gill: this was typified by the high priest bearing the sins of the holy things of the people of Israel when he went into the most holy place, and by the scapegoat bearing the iniquities of all the people unto a land not inhabited, and foretold by the Prophet Isaiah; Christ bore the sins of his people in his own body; Peter declaring that Christ his own self bore our sins in his body on the tree, that we, dead to sins, should live to righteousness, and by his stripes were healed — the substitutionary sin-bearing of Christ and its saving, sanctifying end (Peter, rising from Christ's example to his atonement, setting forth the heart of the gospel: 'who his own self bare our sins in his own body on the tree,' Christ in person carrying the load of his people's sins, bearing their guilt and its penalty in his own body on the cross, the great act of substitution, and the twofold end: 'that we, being dead to sins, should live unto righteousness,' his death procuring our death to sin and life to righteousness, our sanctification, and the healing: 'by whose stripes ye were healed'; the rich truth, that Christ's penal, substitutionary sin-bearing both makes atonement for guilt and frees the believer for holy living, and his very wounds, 'stripes,' are the means of the soul's healing from sin).

debates.question: What did Christ accomplish in bearing our sins on the tree?

sides.position: A penal, substitutionary sin-bearing — Christ in person carried his people's guilt and its penalty in his own body on the cross, making atonement, and his death frees the believer to die to sin and live to righteousness

themes: who his own self bare our sins in his own body on the tree
that we, being dead to sins, should live unto righteousness
by whose stripes ye were healed
the substitutionary sin-bearing of Christ

schools.summary: Gill: this is a proof of their being healed, namely their conversion, in which an application of the blood of Christ and pardon, and so healing by it, was made to their souls; God's elect are sheep before conversion (not that they have the qualities of sheep, but by virtue of electing grace), but as sheep going astray, wandering from God and the fold; Peter declaring that they were as sheep going astray, but are now returned to the Shepherd and Bishop of their souls — the return of the straying sheep to Christ their Shepherd and Overseer (Peter closing the chapter with a tender image of conversion: 'for ye were as sheep going astray,' before grace found them they wandered like lost sheep, straying from God, the fold, and the way, helpless and exposed, 'but are now returned unto the Shepherd and Bishop of your souls'; the blessed change and the titles of Christ, that they have been brought back, by his grace, for sheep do not find their own way home, to Christ who is both the 'Shepherd,' who feeds, guides, and guards his flock, and the 'Bishop,' the Overseer watching over and caring for their souls, so the once-straying are now safe under the tender oversight of Christ, the Shepherd and Guardian of the soul).

themes: for ye were as sheep going astray
but are now returned unto the Shepherd and Bishop of your souls
the return of the straying sheep to Christ
Christ the Shepherd and Overseer of the soul
